title: [parser] opaque errors for incomplete/malformed ^-index sigils (unexpected end of input / coerce empty string)
description:
Found by a dogfooding QA pass of the lexer/parser (msm-0, parser/lexer/CLI lane). The core is solid — escapes (`\ ` `\t` `\n` `\\` `\"`), raw vs interpolating quotes, `$name` interpolation, arithmetic precedence/associativity, assignment (`k=v; $k`), and nested lists all behave correctly. Two ERROR-MESSAGE-QUALITY gaps (not functional bugs) worth polishing:

1. Incomplete role-index sigils (`^*`, `^_`, `^/`, `^`) with NO index produce the opaque `parse error at token 1: unexpected end of input`. These require an index by design (e.g. `^*-1`, `^_-1`, `^/-1`), but the message doesn't say so. A clearer error would be e.g. `message-index sigil '^*' requires an index (e.g. '^*-1')`. Compare the GOOD error for a valid-but-empty index: `^-1` → `no message at ^-1`.

2. A malformed range like `^0^2` yields `cannot coerce string \`\` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number` — an empty-string coercion error that leaks an internal detail instead of naming the malformed range/index.

Both are in the message-index parse path (my M^N range lane, cf bd-c3fc30). Low priority polish: better diagnostics make the `^` indexing surface much friendlier to dogfood. Filed via reflect-session-style QA from an idle dogfooding pass.

Acceptance: incomplete `^*`/`^_`/`^/`/`^` (no index) and malformed `^`-ranges emit a diagnostic that names the sigil + expected index form, rather than `unexpected end of input` / `coerce empty string to number`.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: [nlir][onboarding] checkout refresh persistently fails on ms-mac — git fetch 'No user exists for uid 501', beads stale
description:
## Symptom (routed by log-monitor-msm -> ctrl, 2026-07-03)

nlir (newly onboarded ~13:53 today) has a PERSISTENTLY failing checkout refresh on ms-mac: `POST /api/v1/projects/nlir/checkout/refresh -> 500` (fatal=false) recurring every sweep, keeping nlir's beads STALE (board shows nlir as one of the stale projects).

## Root cause (from ms-mac daemon.log)

    checkout refresh error for nlir (consecutive failures: 40): daemon error: git fetch origin main failed: No user exists for uid 501

40+ consecutive failures. The underlying `git fetch origin main` for nlir's checkout fails with "No user exists for uid 501" (getpwuid(501) failing — uid 501 = the macOS operator user).

## Key discriminator

This is nlir-SPECIFIC: cacophony's checkout refresh on the SAME ms-mac daemon (pid 81113, healthy, ~6h uptime) works fine. So it is NOT a general daemon-env failure — something about nlir's checkout/remote differs.

## NOT the bd-95abff class

This is a git-fetch-ENV error (uid resolution during git fetch), not the bd-95abff silent-materialization-wedge class. Different root; do not fold.

## Fix directions

1. Compare nlir's remote/checkout config vs cacophony's — likely nlir uses a remote type (e.g. SSH) or a git config (core.sshCommand / a credential helper) that triggers a getpwuid(501) lookup the daemon's launchd git-subprocess env can't satisfy, whereas cacophony's remote doesn't.
2. If it's a daemon git-subprocess env gap (missing HOME/USER for the fetch), `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` is inherited by the daemon's child git/subprocess operations (per AGENTS.md) so HOME/USER could be set there — but confirm WHY cacophony is unaffected first before a blanket env change.
3. This is a NEW-PROJECT ONBOARDING settling issue — the nlir owner / config-onboarder (Harry onboarded it ~13:53) should verify nlir's remote + checkout config.

Non-fatal, nlir-specific, ms-mac daemon otherwise healthy. Routed by log-monitor-msm; ctrl found the root cause + filed. cc Harry (nlir onboarder).

## Diagnostic (msm-0, read-only, 2026-07-03 ~19:40)

Refutes fix-direction #1 and narrows to a daemon env bug:

- **Both nlir AND cacophony use `ssh://git@github.com/...` remotes** (checked `caco config show --section projects`). Not a remote-type difference.
- **Both daemon canonical checkouts (`~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/{nlir,cacophony}`) have IDENTICAL local git config**: same `core.sshCommand = ssh -F /dev/null -i ~/.ssh/caco -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new`, same `remote.origin.url` class, and BOTH have local `user.name`/`user.email` set. So the failure is NOT a checkout-config difference.
- **nlir's checkout is proven functional**: `git -C ~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/nlir ls-remote origin main` (HOME set, normal env) returns the ref cleanly. The key/remote/config all work.
- The `No user exists for uid 501` is git's `getpwuid(501)` failing to resolve HOME when HOME is unset in the fetch subprocess. Since cacophony succeeds on the SAME daemon with IDENTICAL config, this is a **daemon env-propagation bug**: the git-fetch subprocess for the newly-onboarded nlir project is missing HOME (existing projects like cacophony inherit it; a project onboarded mid-daemon-run at ~13:53 apparently does not).

### Recommended fix (daemon/operator lane — NOT nlir checkout)
1. Simplest: **restart the ms-mac caco-daemon** so nlir's fetch subprocess inherits HOME like cacophony's (likely clears the 40-consec-failure state, since onboarding mid-run didn't propagate env).
2. If it persists after restart: set `HOME=/Users/harryaskham` (and `USER`) in `services.caco-daemon-extra-env` so every daemon git subprocess has it, or fix the onboarding path to propagate the daemon's env to per-project fetch subprocesses.

nlir's checkout/remote/config need no change. Handing to Harry/ctrl (daemon owners).

title: llm-mode operand coercion doesn't use the LLM coercion fallback (eval uses deterministic-only Value::coerce)
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive). In `--mode llm`, operand coercion does NOT use the LLM coercion fallback, so a non-numeric operand to a number operator errors instead of being interpreted by the model.

Repro: `nlir --mode llm -e "'ten'+'twenty'"` -> "cannot coerce string `ten` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number" (exit 1). SPEC explicitly wants this to work: "an LLM coercion — interpret the text as a value of type T ... turns a vague 'ten to twenty' into 15." Expected: 'ten'->10, 'twenty'->20, add -> 30.

Root cause: src/eval.rs coerces operands via `Value::coerce(target, sep)` (the deterministic-only path, bd-20df97) at all 4 coercion sites (lines ~257, 344, 560, 614), never `llm::coerce_with_llm` (the deterministic->LLM->error fallback, bd-ecb930). So step-3 LLM coercion from the config `types:` map is never invoked in llm mode. The misleading error text ("or model coercion") comes from Value::coerce's CoerceError::Unrepresentable, which describes the model step even though Value::coerce never runs it.

This is the eval<->coercion seam (mirrors the realise_llm seam bd-dc3c72): aur-2 owns the coercion API (coerce_with_llm), aur-1 owns eval.rs. Proposed fix: aur-2 adds `llm::coerce_operand(value, target, &Config, sep, mode, env_lookup, cli_model) -> Result<Value, LlmCoerceError>` that dispatches det -> Value::coerce, llm -> coerce_with_llm; eval.rs adds EvalError::LlmCoerce + From<LlmCoerceError> and swaps the 4 `.coerce(...)` sites to it (mode-aware). Det mode behaviour unchanged.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: [operator-action][infra] darwin CI runner: job steps can't produce a portable native build (restricted env + nix evals non-darwin system)
description:
The aarch64-darwin release build cannot produce a portable binary from CI because of the self-hosted nix github-runner's job-step environment. Diagnosed exhaustively during bd-7e465a (ms-mac, run 28708607800 and predecessors).

Findings (confirmed on the runner + via log co-reads):
1. Job steps run in a RESTRICTED env: a service user with HOME=/var/empty (echo `lh=/var/empty`), a minimal PATH without /usr/bin, so the system Apple toolchain /usr/bin/cc is unreachable (`cc=none` even after an unconditional `export PATH=/usr/bin:/bin:$PATH`).
2. The login user's rustup toolchain at /Users/harryaskham/.cargo|.rustup is unreadable to the job user (/Users/harryaskham is 0750).
3. So the ONLY reachable toolchain is the nix store. Building inside the flake devShell (`nix develop --command cargo build`) DOES compile nlir on the runner (2m06s). BUT the runner's `nix develop` resolves a NON-darwin devShell (it copies stdenv-linux + gcc-wrapper-14.3.0), whereas the SAME flake on the same aarch64-darwin host locally resolves clang-wrapper-21 + cctools. Result: the devShell lacks darwin cctools, so the portability post-step fails with `otool: command not found` (exit 127). `nix eval builtins.currentSystem` locally = aarch64-darwin.

So the runner's nix is evaluating a non-aarch64-darwin system for job steps. release.yml cannot work around this cleanly.

Proposed fixes (pick one):
(a) Run darwin runner job steps as the login user (harryaskham) with /usr/bin + Xcode CLT on PATH, so the existing native-cargo (rust-not-nix) release.yml works and yields a portable binary. Simplest to reason about.
(b) Fix the runner's nix so `nix develop`/builds evaluate aarch64-darwin (clang-wrapper + cctools). Then the devShell + `install_name_tool` libiconv-rewrite approach (already in release.yml, bd-7e465a) completes.

Interim already available: a portable nlir-0.1.1-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz was built + verified locally on ms-mac (otool shows only /usr/lib/libiconv.2.dylib + libSystem; runs `nlir 0.1.1`; sha256 5ee3319e9e78aed0535f33c0e6c5fb4d18ed34a749095f1eb78e0da443d9fa2a) and can be attached to v0.1.1.

Acceptance: a release.yml -f tag=v0.1.x dispatch produces + attaches nlir-<v>-aarch64-darwin.tar.gz whose `otool -L` shows only /usr/lib deps, from CI, with no manual step.

UPDATE (msm-0, further diagnosis): The workflow layer is exhausted. Landed the explicit-devShell fix (`nix develop '.#devShells.aarch64-darwin.default'`, adcf099) to force the right system — that CORRECTLY targets aarch64-darwin, but it surfaced the true blocker: the github-runner's nix cannot BUILD the darwin flake. Run 28719755717 darwin job: `error: Cannot build …-feedback-cli-patched.drv` → `error: Cannot build …-nlir-src.drv` → `… __impureHostDeps of derivation 'nix-shell'` → `error: Build failed due to failed dependency`.

Two different nixes on the one Mac: the github-runner SERVICE nix is 2.34.7 (`system=aarch64-darwin`, `extra-platforms=aarch64-darwin x86_64-darwin`, `builders=@/etc/nix/machines`) and FAILS to build the darwin devShell (a dep, feedback-cli-patched, won't build; __impureHostDeps darwin-sandbox host-deps). The login/interactive nix is Determinate Nix 3.13.2 and builds the identical flake devShell fine (verified locally: 2m49s, portable binary, otool clean).

So the infra fix narrows to: make the github-runner build darwin like the interactive Determinate nix — point the runner service at Determinate Nix 3.13.2, OR fix its darwin sandbox / __impureHostDeps / /etc/nix/machines builder config so darwin derivations build locally. Once the runner can build the darwin devShell, the landed `.#devShells.aarch64-darwin.default` + otool libiconv-rewrite completes and yields a portable CI asset. Interim locally-built asset (Determinate nix, sha 5ee3319e…) remains ready to attach.

UPDATE 2 (msm-0): `--builders ''` was INSUFFICIENT. Re-dispatch (run 28721262361, commit 22f1279) applied the flag (confirmed in job log line 171) but failed IDENTICALLY: `Cannot build feedback-cli-patched.drv` → `nlir-src.drv` → `__impureHostDeps of nix-shell` → `Build failed due to failed dependency`. So it is NOT builder-delegation — the restricted CI job env (service user, HOME=/var/empty) cannot build the flake dep feedback-cli-patched (network/substituter/sandbox), dying at eval of the devShell's __impureHostDeps. In-workflow fixes are now exhausted (explicit devShell + --builders '' both landed, both correct, neither sufficient in the restricted env). Durable fix is runner-level (aur-0's path): ~/collective/modules/home/git.nix runner devshell / service env (network + darwin build capability) + darwin-rebuild. Interim locally-built portable asset (sha 5ee3319e…) remains the fast unblock.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Decide where MessageViews SPEC defaults live (config resolve vs messages fallback)
description: SPEC §Example config defines canonical `context.messages.views` defaults (`^`=[assistant], `^_`=[user], `^*`=[user,assistant,system], `^/`=[system]), but config `MessageViews::default()` is all-empty and `resolve_defaults` (src/config.rs) does not populate them. So the built-in `^` behaviour (and the SPEC `msg` test `^-1`) only works because src/messages.rs `effective_roles()` bakes those SPEC defaults as a fallback for an empty configured view. That's a reasonable seam, but it splits "view defaults" across two modules. Decision for the config owner: either (a) centralize the SPEC view defaults in config defaults-resolution (then messages could drop its fallback), or (b) keep them in the messages layer and document config `MessageViews::default()` as intentionally empty. Either is fine; flagging so the two don't silently diverge. Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-f9809a/bd-e8064e/bd-43ac5e session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Parallelism may not concurrency-run independent llm operand subcalls (30s for 4-way; investigate scheduler vs HTTP-client mutex vs endpoint)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode: independent LLM operand subtrees do NOT appear to run concurrently despite the parallelism epic (bd-a32894, std::thread::scope, defaults.parallelism=8). Evidence (anthropic_messages HTTP backend against a real endpoint):
- single `!('the sky is blue')` ≈ 4s
- `!(a)&!(b)` (2 independent ! + &) ≈ 14s
- `!(a)&!(b)&!(c)&!(d)` (4 independent ! + &) ≈ 30s

30s ≈ 5 sequential ~5s calls; concurrent would be ~2 call-times (~10s). So the 4 independent `!` subcalls are running sequentially, not concurrently.

Needs investigation (aur-1's parallelism lane): does the scheduler parallelize the operands of a single n-ary Apply (the `&` operands)? Is the anthropic_messages ureq HTTP client behind a shared mutex that serializes? Or does the endpoint rate-limit? Reproduce with a command-backend model (claude CLI) to rule out endpoint serialization. Config: defaults.parallelism=8, per-operand `!` uses model sonnet (anthropic_messages). Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library; may be endpoint-side, hence P3 pending confirmation.

## Refined diagnosis (msm-0 + aur-1, 2026-07-03)

NOT a scheduler bug. Confirmed the scheduler + COMMAND backends DO parallelize:
- A `command:` operator that `sleep 2`s: 4-way `~a&~b&~c&~d` = 2.1s (not 8s) — concurrent.
- aur-1: 3 concurrent subject extractions via command (claude/pi CLI) joined ~13s — parallelism live.

So the ~30s serialization I saw is SPECIFIC to the `anthropic_messages` HTTP path against the LiteLLM endpoint (helsinki:4000). nlir uses a FRESH `ureq::post` per call (no shared client/Mutex in llm.rs), so it is NOT a nlir lock. Most likely LiteLLM/Claude endpoint concurrency limits serialize the concurrent HTTP requests, or ureq's default agent behavior. Downgrading: probably endpoint-side, not a nlir bug. To fully confirm nlir fires concurrent HTTP, a local sleeping mock-HTTP anthropic server would settle it. Separately noticed `--parallelism 1` did not force sequential in the sleep test (flag→scheduler wiring gap worth a small check).

## RESOLVED — direct HTTP path DOES parallelize (aur-1, real-endpoint evidence)

Tested the anthropic_messages HTTP backend against the LiteLLM endpoint at the CORRECT URL: http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000/v1 (note: the operator's given URL http://.../v1 was missing the :4000 port → port 80 connection-refused from aurora; LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://helsinki.miku-owl.ts.net:4000). Config defaults.model=direct, op with no model override (so the direct backend actually runs, not a command backend).

Clean before/after timing (3 concurrent subject extractions + join):
- single #'x' baseline = 4s
- #'a'&#'b'&#'c' at parallelism 8 = 8s (~2x baseline = 3 subjects CONCURRENT + 1 join)
- same at --parallelism 1 = 17s (~4x baseline = fully sequential)

8s vs 17s => the direct HTTP path parallelizes correctly. The scheduler parallelizes ALL backends (command AND anthropic_messages HTTP). The earlier ~30s 4-way observation was transient endpoint behaviour (cold/loaded), NOT a systematic nlir serialization or client-mutex bug. No mock-HTTP server needed — real-endpoint data settles it. Closing.

(The --parallelism-flag-not-wired sub-note was split to bd-e9dedc and is LANDED.)
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: nlir repl: :step interactive step-through UX (crossterm shell over the step engine)
description:
Harry ask #1 of 2 (sgu24-app 2026-07-05): "in nlir repl, pressing tab to expand only the next step of the eval and inline it — see it realtime, learn the lang."

Scope (aur-0, the REPL UX half; msm-0 owns the step ENGINE bd-9c366d): a `:step EXPR` REPL meta-command that enters a raw-mode (crossterm, TTY-gated) step viewer — Tab/Space expands the next eval step and reprints the partially-reduced expression inline, Enter runs to completion, q/Esc/Ctrl-C cancels. Non-TTY (pipes/tests) degrades to a single full evaluation so scripted REPL use is unchanged.

Seam: msm-0's `Evaluator::step_once(&mut self, &Expr) -> Step{Reduced(Expr),Done(Value)}` + precedence-aware `Expr::render_step()` (landing to main independently, ⊥ the held Δ+bare-views grammar). Foundation (parse, TTY-gate, non-TTY fallback, crossterm key handling, `:step` wiring, crossterm 0.28 dep) is built + green; wiring the real step_once is a localized swap (a reprint loop with `let mut cur` + `.render_step()`).

Parent: REPL epic bd-c6719d.

title: Concurrent in-flight subcall dedup (two simultaneous identical LLM calls both compute)
description: The per-run realisation cache (bd-1d078c) is a Mutex<HashMap> that dedups SEQUENTIAL identical subcalls, but two truly-simultaneous identical subcalls on different scoped threads (bd-780dbf) may BOTH miss the cache before either stores, so both compute — no in-flight dedup. For pure det ops this is harmless (same result); for identical concurrent LLM calls it means a duplicate model call. Fix: an in-flight registry keyed by the realise cache key holding a shared "computing" placeholder (Condvar/OnceLock) so a second thread waits for the first's result. Low priority; correctness holds either way. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf/bd-1d078c parallelism session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: Wire double-quote $name interpolation into eval (lexer must carry the quote kind)
description:
SPEC §Interpolation: a double-quoted string `"…$name…"` interpolates bare `$name` at eval time; single-quoted `'…'` (raw) and bare literals do not. The interpolation FUNCTION exists (context::interpolate, bd-22fa7e), but it is NOT wired into the evaluator, so `nlir -e 'k=x;"v is $k"'` yields the literal "v is $k" instead of "v is x".

Root cause: the lexer collapses both `"…"` and `'…'` into a single `Token::Quoted(String)` with no flag distinguishing interpolating (double) from raw (single) quotes, so the parser's `Expr::Quoted` can't tell eval whether to interpolate.

Fix (cross-lane):
1. Lexer (msm-0's lane): carry the quote kind — e.g. `Token::Quoted { content, interpolate: bool }` (true for `"…"`, false for `'…'`) or a distinct token — and likewise `Expr::Quoted`/a new `Expr::Interpolated` in the parser.
2. Eval (aur-1): in eval, call `self.context.interpolate(&content)` for interpolating strings (I already have Context::interpolate); leave raw/bare literal.

Discovered by aur-1 while adding greedy eval-time read tests (bd-91e573). I can do the eval-side wiring once the lexer/parser carries the interpolate flag.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: config.example.yaml command models: YAML folded-scalar keeps newlines, breaking bash command backends
description:
Found while dogfooding nlir in LLM mode (Harry's directive, 2026-07-03). The shipped config.example.yaml `command:` model fields use a YAML `>` folded scalar with MORE-INDENTED continuation lines, e.g.:

  command: >
    claude --model claude-sonnet-5
      --system-prompt "..."
      --print "..."

Per the YAML folded-scalar spec, lines indented more than the block's first content line are kept as LITERAL newlines (the "more-indented" exception) rather than folded to spaces. So the resulting command string has real newlines, and `bash -c` runs each line as a SEPARATE statement: `claude --model claude-sonnet-5` (no prompt), then `--print "..."` -> "bash: --print: command not found" (exit 127). Any command-model realisation from the shipped example is broken.

Repro: nlir --mode llm -e "#'the quick brown fox'" with a claude command model authored via `>` folded + indented continuations -> "command backend exited with status 127: ... --print: command not found".

FIX: author command: fields as SINGLE-LINE commands (or a `|` literal block that is valid multi-line bash). Note the echo operator's `command: |` literal block is CORRECT (intentional multi-line bash); the bug is specifically the `>` folded style with unintended-newline continuations. Also update the models to Harry's requested real backends: claude (claude-sonnet-5 via the claude binary) + copilot (github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6 via pi; copilot has no claude-sonnet-5 on the Responses API). Keep example_config_parses_and_validates green.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Dedup negative-index-from-end resolver (stack + messages resolve_index)
description: `Stack::resolve_index` (src/stack.rs) and `messages::resolve_index` (src/messages.rs) are the identical negative-index-from-end resolver: `index >= 0` from the start, `index < 0` from the end (`-1` = last), `None` out of range. Both back SPEC index semantics (`$N` stack index with `$0` bottom / `$-1` top; `^N` message index). A shared helper (e.g. `fn resolve_index(len, i64) -> Option<usize>` in a small `index`/util module, or one made pub and reused) would dedup ~6 lines and keep the semantics in one place. Low priority; the two are currently in-sync. Filed via reflect-session mixin from bd-d4631b/bd-22fa7e session.

title: nlir power-move #21: the myth-buster @&[!'MISCONCEPTION','REALITY'] — correct the record
description:
nlir power-move #21 (aur-2) — "the myth-buster" (correct the record): reject a common misconception AND state what's actually true, woven into one authoritative correction. Builds purposefully on the ! operator in a standalone way nobody had.

SHAPE: @&[!'MISCONCEPTION', 'THE_REALITY'] — the ! slot flips the false claim, the next slot gives the reality, & weaves them "not X; rather Y". Reads NO chat (busts a free-standing myth) — distinct from aur-1's reasoned-no @(!^-1 & grounds) which rejects THEIR last message. Use : instead of @ to correct gently.

WHY ! MATTERS (verified A/B): without it, @&['microservices always scale better',...] ASSERTS the myth; with it, @&[!'microservices always scale better',...] -> "Microservices do NOT invariably scale better..." The ! is the difference between repeating a myth and busting it.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): @&[!'microservices always scale better than monoliths','they trade operational complexity for independent scaling, worth it only past a certain team size'] -> "Microservices do not invariably scale better than monolithic architectures; rather, they exchange increased operational complexity for the benefit of independent scaling, a trade-off that is justified only beyond a certain organizational size."

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-mythbuster.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-myth-buster.png (my 11th card; short more-tests example; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row.

title: [lexer] Scientific-notation float literals do not lex: 1.5e3 / 1e-9 rejected
description:
Found by driving nlir (dogfooding), sibling of bd-f551f9 (bare float literals).

Scientific-notation number literals are inconsistently supported:

- `1e5` / `2e2` lex fine (all-alphanumeric bare token) and coerce in numeric context (`1e3 + 1` -> 1001, `2e2 * 2` -> 400).
- `1.5e3`, `6.022e23`, `1.0e0` -> `parse error at token 1: unexpected token Bare("e3") after statement`. The lexer float extension (bd-f551f9) reads `1.5` then stops at `e`, leaving `e3` as a stray bare token.
- `1e-9`, `2e-3` (negative exponent) -> the alphanumeric run stops at the `-` leaving `2e`, which then fails to coerce: `cannot coerce string "2e" to number`.

Root cause: purely a LEXER gap. `Value::coerce_deterministic` already uses `s.trim().parse::<f64>()`, which understands the full exponent form — so once the literal lexes as ONE bare token it coerces correctly with no parser/eval change (same pattern as bd-f551f9's `.` float support).

Fix: extend `lex_bare`'s numeric tail to absorb a scientific-notation exponent `[eE][+-]?<digits>` onto a numeric mantissa (`<digits>` or `<digits>.<digits>`). Two shapes reach the tail because the alnum run treats `e`/`E` as an identifier char but stops at the sign: `1.5e3` (sits AT the `e`) and `1e-9` (run already pulled `e` in, sits at the sign). Both mantissas are unambiguously numeric (`1.5e`/`1e` is not a valid identifier), so absorbing never steals a `-`/`+` subtraction operator. Spaced forms (`1e - 9`) and non-numeric mantissas (`abce-3`) are unaffected.

Acceptance: `1.5e3`, `6.022e23`, `1e-9`, `2.5e+2` lex as one bare number and evaluate numerically in det mode; identifiers ending in `e` and `<digits>-<digits>` subtraction unchanged.

title: nlir coercion: hex/binary/thousands/percent/fraction coerce OFFLINE in det mode (no LLM round-trip)
description:
nlir coercion enhancement (aur-2 types/coercion lane; aur-0's dogfooding find). In --mode det, hex/binary/thousands/percent/fraction FELL THROUGH to the LLM despite being trivially deterministic — an unnecessary model round-trip for forms the CATALOG already claims as coercions.

Fix (src/value.rs): extracted parse_number_str(), tried in coerce_deterministic's Number branch BEFORE the model fallback: (1) plain float (unchanged: 42/3.5/-5/1e3/+3) → (2) hex 0x / binary 0b integer literals (optional sign) → (3) comma-grouped thousands 1,000 → (4) percent 50%->0.5 → (5) simple fraction 1/2->0.5 (guards divide-by-zero). Returns None for richer text (spelled numbers, ranges) so it still defers to the LLM/loud-error layer. Backward-compatible (only reached when plain parse fails).

Verified OFFLINE in det mode (no key): '0xFF'+'1'=256, '1,000'+'1'=1001, '50%'+'50%'=1, '1/2'+'1/2'=1, '0b101'+'1'=6, '3/4'*'4'=3. Full CI gate GREEN: cargo fmt --check + clippy --all-targets -D warnings + test --all (new test coerce_string_to_number_handles_offline_special_forms passes) + config::tests::example_config + nlir test 17/17. Now these forms are offline, zero LLM calls, faster + variance-free. Credit aur-0 for the find.

title: Division renders full f64 precision (10/3 -> 3.3333333333333335) — decide display precision
description:
Found dogfooding. Non-terminating division renders at full f64 precision, which is ugly for an English-output IR: `nlir --mode det -e '10/3'` -> "3.3333333333333335". Clean fractions are fine (1/2 -> 0.5, 7/2 -> 3.5, 100/8 -> 12.5); only repeating decimals surface the raw f64 shortest-round-trip string.

Location: Value::format_number in src/value.rs (aur-2 lane). This is a design decision, not a clear bug — options: (a) leave as-is (exact, deterministic, round-trippable); (b) round non-integral results to N significant figures for display (loses precision, changes value semantics if the rounded value feeds further ops); (c) only round at final stdout render, keeping full precision internally. SPEC doesn't specify float display precision and has no division examples. Filing as a draft for team/operator input before changing value semantics. Related: bd-f551f9 (bare float literals like 3.14 don't lex yet — msm-0 lexer lane); a display-precision decision should be consistent with literal float support.

SCOPE NOTE (2026-07-04, via aur-0): the full-f64-precision display question covers LARGE INTEGERS too, not just non-terminating division. nlir numbers are f64 formatted in shortest-round-trip form, so integers are bit-exact only up to 2^53; above that they're f64 approximations that SOMETIMES look clean (10^100, 10^20 print as clean 1+zeros) and SOMETIMES show visible noise (10**23 => 100000000000000010000000; 2**53+1 == 2**53; 2**63 => 9223372036854776000, true value ends ...5808). Any future "exact big-integer" or precision-display work should treat large-int display and non-terminating fractions as the same underlying f64-representation question. Surfaced while correcting the golf-aur2-googol.sh example header (which had a misleading "printed exactly, no rounding" claim, now fixed).

title: Project skeleton: Nix flake + Rust `nlir` CLI with crate deps wired
description:

title: bug: \$ escape defeated in double-quote interpolation ("\$k" interpolates instead of literal $k)
description:
DOGFOOD FINDING (msm0). In double-quoted strings, the `\$` escape does NOT produce a literal `$` when followed by a valid variable name — it interpolates instead.

REPRO (det mode, config.example.yaml):
  k=world;"\$k"   => "world"   (WRONG — want literal "$k")
  k=world;"\$5"   => "$5"      (works, but only coincidentally: $5 is digit-led so never interpolates anyway)

ROOT CAUSE (two-stage processing):
1. LEXER src/lexer.rs read_escape() (~line 497): the `other => other` arm collapses ANY unrecognised escape to the bare char, so `\$` -> `$`. The lexed content of "\$k" becomes `$k` with interpolate=true.
2. context.rs interpolate() (~line 411) then runs on `$k`, sees `$` + alphabetic, and interpolates it. It cannot tell an escaped `$` from a real one — the `\` was already eaten by the lexer.

So the escape is defeated by de-escaping BEFORE interpolation. Per lexer docs ("`"…"` with POSIX escapes processed; $name interpolation applied at eval time"), `\$` should be a literal `$` and must NOT interpolate.

PROPOSED FIX (spans lexer + interpolate; this is msm0's interpolation feature, bd-2a1cb6):
- LEXER (msm0 lane): in lex_escaped_quote, special-case `\$` to PRESERVE it as the two chars `\$` in the content (do not call read_escape's collapsing path for `$`), leaving the escape intact for eval-time interpolation. Keep `\n`/`\t`/`\"`/`\\` collapsing unchanged.
- INTERPOLATE (context.rs, shared): add a branch so `\$` emits a literal `$` and skips interpolation of the following name (like shells). i.e. when the scanner sees `\` followed by `$`, consume both and push `$`.

REGRESSION GUARDS: `k=world;"\$k"` == literal "$k"; `k=world;"$k"` == "world" (unchanged); `"a\nb"` still newline; `"\\"` still backslash. Also verify raw single-quote `'$k'` stays literal (already correct).

msm0 owns the interpolation feature and can implement both halves; coordinating the context.rs touch. Cosmetic-adjacent but a real correctness bug: you currently cannot put a literal `$var`-shaped string through double quotes.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Math precedence broken by default: default_priority ignores the SPEC ladder for arithmetic sigils
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. parser::default_priority only keys off FIXITY (prefix 14, postfix 1, infix/mixfix = DEFAULT_PRIORITY 9). So a config that declares the canonical arithmetic operators WITHOUT explicit `priority:` gets +, *, ** all at priority 9 → `1+2*3` evaluates to 9, not 7. The SPEC §Grammar precedence ladder (** 13, * / 12, + - 11, & | 9) is documented as the intended precedence but is NOT applied by default, and the SPEC §Example config `operators:` block omits explicit priorities on add/mul/sub/div/pow — so a config author copying the SPEC example gets mathematically-wrong precedence.

Verified: with explicit `priority: 11/12/13` on +-/*//** the parser respects them correctly (1+2*3=7, 2**3*2=16, 2+3*4-1=13). So the parser is fine; the gap is the DEFAULTS + the SPEC example.

Options: (a) update the SPEC §Example config to include the precedence ladder priorities on the arithmetic ops (minimal); (b) add a sigil-aware default-priority resolution for the canonical arithmetic sigils (**,*,/,+,-) so the SPEC ladder holds without explicit config (more magic, less config-driven); (c) document loudly that arithmetic operators MUST set priority. Filed by aur-1 from the nlir LLM-mode driving session.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: [design] Unary minus / negative literals — should nlir support prefix `-` (negative numbers + negative-index exprs)?
description:
Design question for Harry (flagged by msm-0, filed by aur-0 with msm-0's go-ahead). Should nlir support unary minus / negative literals?

Today negatives are only reachable via:
- binary subtraction from a literal: `0-5` => -5
- computed expressions: `(0-1)**0.5`, `(0-1)^*0` (a computed negative index)

There is NO unary-minus / negative-literal syntax, so:
- `-5` does not evaluate (`-` is infix-only: "operator \"-\" is not valid in prefix position")
- `5*-2` fails (no unary minus after an operator)
- a range/index expression that opens with a negative index, e.g. `-1^*0`, reaches the evaluator (after CLI bd-d2d23c's allow_hyphen_values fix) but then hits the same prefix-`-` parser limit
- negative number literals like `-3.14` cannot be written directly

This is a real LANGUAGE FEATURE, deliberately NOT bundled into the CLI paper-cut bd-d2d23c (that only fixed clap eating a leading `-`). It needs a design + SPEC decision, not an overnight ride-along:
- Add a dual-role prefix/infix `-` to the parser (unary negate when in prefix position, binary subtract when infix), OR
- Support negative numeric literals in the lexer (`-5`, `-3.14`) and negative-index literals, OR
- Decide the current 0-N / (0-1)^*N idioms are sufficient and leave `-` infix-only (document it explicitly in SPEC).

Tradeoffs to weigh: parser ambiguity between prefix-negate and infix-subtract (precedence/associativity), interaction with the `^N` negative-index reads (which already accept `-1` as an index token in that position), and SPEC's current "negatives come from 0-N" implicit stance. Lexer vs parser split (negative literal token vs prefix operator) is itself part of the design.

Lane: parser (msm-0) — draft/low-pri, awaiting Harry's SPEC call before it becomes queued work. Related: bd-d2d23c (CLI leading-hyphen, closed), bd-f551f9 (float literals, closed — the lexer-number-shape cousin).

title: [broken-on-main] CI ensure-curl/rustup step exits 1 under set -e (final short-circuited test), reddening main
description:
main CI is RED: the "Ensure curl and rustup are reachable for the toolchain install (minimal step PATH)" step (added by my curl+rustup PATH fix, landed 1fda55b) fails on the build+test job, skipping every downstream step. Routed to me by aur-0, who is HOLDING the v0.1.2 cut on a red main.

Failing run: 28705697657 @cb8d62d -> `##[error]Process completed with exit code 1`.

Root cause (confirmed from the step log): the run shell is `bash --noprofile --norc -e -o pipefail` (set -e). The step body ends with `[ -x "$d/rustup" ] && echo "$d" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"`. On the azure-ephemeral runner rustup is not installed yet (that is exactly what the next dtolnay step does), so the final `[ -x ]` test returns non-zero, the `&&` short-circuits, and that becomes the SCRIPT's exit code -> the step fails even though the PATH appends all succeeded. Same shape for the first loop's `/opt/homebrew/bin` on Linux. It is not a mid-script set -e trip (those `&&` lists are exempt) — it is purely the final exit code.

Fix: make this best-effort PATH step incapable of failing the job — convert `[ ... ] && echo` to `if [ ... ]; then echo ...; fi` (set -e-safe) and end with `exit 0`. Verified locally under `bash -e -o pipefail` with homebrew/rustup absent: OLD exits 1, NEW exits 0, both append the identical real dirs. Applies to all three identical step bodies: ci.yml (build+test) and release.yml (both jobs).

Acceptance: main ci.yml build+test goes green; a release run's aarch64-darwin leg passes; then aur-0 cuts v0.1.2.

title: nlir docs: op[list]/op^ = op-on-rendered-text (not a structural map) — correct changelog #25 + POWERMOVES list-algebra note
description:
nlir docs precision (aur-2) — swarm convergence refining my changelog #25 "op[list] MAPS" finding. aur-0 (DET mechanism) + aur-1 (op-specific) + msm-0 (message ranges) converged: op[list] = the op applied to the list RENDERED AS TEXT — never a structural map. Verified DET: !['a','b','c'] = "not a\nb\nc" (\"not \" prepended ONCE to the multi-line render), !&['a','b','c'] = "not a and b and c" (structural weave). In LLM mode the model guesses per-op over the text blob: only :[list] reliably maps per-item, @[list]/>[list] are non-deterministic (bloom-last/weave), reductive #/~/<[list] FOLD to one, &[list] WEAVES structurally. msm-0: message RANGES are the SAME algebra (op^* likewise op-on-rendered-text). Upgrades the proposed MAP ↦ to a concretely-justified structural gap (the only guaranteed per-item/per-message map — @↦ formal changelog, @↦^_ formalise each turn).

Fix: corrected examples/move-aur2-changelog.sh register-dial advice (only : reliably maps; @/~ over a list do NOT give a formal/terse changelog) + added a canonical "List algebra" note to examples/POWERMOVES.md COMPOSE section (lists AND ranges). title: fresh agent checkouts lack a git author identity for direct commits
description:
A managed agent checkout has no git `user.name`/`user.email` configured, so the
first direct `git commit` a dev worker makes fails with "Author identity
unknown ... Please tell me who you are" until the agent sets a local identity.
Prior mainline commits are authored `Cacophony Daemon <daemon@cacophony.local>`,
so workers must discover and replicate that convention by hand. Suggested fix:
seed a repo-local git identity (matching the daemon convention) at checkout
creation, or document the expected identity in AGENTS/SPEC. May be a
cacophony-harness concern rather than nlir-specific. Filed via reflect-session
mixin from bd-700306 session.

## Note (msm-0, 2026-07-03 ~19:55)

Current state: managed nlir checkouts now DO carry a repo-local git identity — both my agent checkout and the daemon canonical checkouts (`~/.cacophony/daemon/checkouts/{nlir,cacophony}`) have `user.name`/`user.email` set locally, and a global `~/.gitconfig` identity also exists as a fallback. So direct `git commit` works today; this appears resolved at the harness level since the 16:27 filing. Leaving open as a harness/dx concern for aur-2 / the cacophony-harness owner to confirm the seeding is intentional + universal (fresh checkouts on a host without a global identity). Not an nlir-repo code change.

description: Harry asked nlir to ship a pi PACKAGE (like agent-utils) so `packages: ["git:github.com/harryaskham/nlir"]` in pi settings gives the `|expr` nlir-expansion inside pi. title: llm-mode coercion bypasses type rules: list->number is LLM-coerced (invents a number) instead of a hard error
description:
Found dogfooding nlir in LLM mode. `nlir --mode llm -e "[1,2]+3"` returns 6 instead of a loud error. Per SPEC (Types & coercion) and the landed coercion layer (bd-20df97), `list -> number` is ALWAYS an error and must never be routed to the LLM. But eval's coerce_operand (src/eval.rs, from bd-ba9f85) handles the llm branch by calling llm::realise_llm directly with the types.number model+prompt, which asks the model to interpret the rendered list ("1\n2") as a number -> it invents one (here 3), so [1,2]+3 -> 6. This violates the type-system hard rule and silently produces a wrong result.

Root cause: coerce_operand reimplements the coercion flow (deterministic -> realise_llm) instead of delegating to the types-layer coercion API (llm::coerce_with_llm, bd-ecb930), which enforces the list->number hard error and the deterministic/loud-error rules. realise_llm is the OPERATOR-realisation seam, not the coercion seam.

Fix options: (a) make coerce_operand call llm::coerce_with_llm in llm mode (it already does deterministic-first, list->number hard error, model coercion via the types.model/prompt, and UnparseableResult on a non-T answer) instead of realise_llm; or (b) add an explicit list->number (and other never-LLM) guard in coerce_operand before the realise_llm call. Option (a) also fixes: caching (coerce_with_llm can front the CoercionCache bd-876367 -- coercions are currently NOT cached), and correct loud errors when the model returns a non-number. eval.rs is msm-0/aur-1 lane; this is the types-coercion semantics (aur-2 lane) -- coordinate the wire like the realise_llm seam.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2

title: Nested parallelization (parallelize deeply-nested independent subcalls, not just top-level)
description: The parallel DAG scheduler (bd-780dbf) parallelizes only the TOP-LEVEL operands of each Apply: a parallelized operand subtree evaluates SEQUENTIALLY inside (eval_parallel_safe does not spawn nested threads). This bounds total concurrency simply (max ~parallelism per Apply) but does not run deeply-nested independent LLM/command calls concurrently — e.g. in `(#a&#b) & (#c&#d)` the inner pairs run sequentially within their outer-operand thread. A nested scheduler (a shared bounded semaphore/pool across recursion, or a work-stealing queue) would lift this while keeping the global parallelism bound. Also: once nested parallelization exists, the backtick Serial handling (bd-f66c32) becomes load-bearing (it must prevent nested parallelization inside a `\`` subtree) — currently vacuous since nothing nests. Filed via reflect-session from the bd-780dbf parallelism session.

title: REPL step-through: Tab to expand the next eval step inline (learn-the-language)
description:
Harry request (2026-07-05): in the nlir REPL, pressing Tab expands only the NEXT step of the eval and inlines it — step through an expression's expansion in realtime, as a way of seeing/learning how nlir evaluates. e.g. `~[@a, @b]` → Tab → `~[<formal a>, @b]` → Tab → `~[<formal a>, <formal b>]` → Tab → `<summary>`.

Scope: needs single-step evaluation (the eval currently runs to completion) — expose one reduction step (innermost/leftmost redex) + substitute inline, and a REPL Tab keybinding that advances one step + reprints. Fits the REPL epic (bd-c6719d). Bigger than nlir-help: touches eval (step exposure) + REPL UI. Deterministic ops step cleanly; LLM ops realise one node per step.

Lane: parser/eval/REPL (msm-0), or pair with types/eval. Open for divvy.

PROGRESS (2026-07-05): step-through ENGINE + non-interactive `nlir step` LANDED on main @6771c0c (independent of the held Δ+bare-views grammar). `Evaluator::step_once(&Expr)->Step{Reduced(Expr),Done(Value)}` (leftmost-innermost redex, `Expr::Value` splice), `eval::step_trace()`, `Expr::render_step()`, `nlir step 'EXPR'` subcommand. det `2+3*4`→`2 + (3 * 4)`→`2 + «12»`→`«14»`; llm `~[@a,@b]` unfolds one realise/step. 215 lib tests (2 new step tests). REMAINING (aur-0, in progress): interactive crossterm raw-mode `:step` REPL Tab UX (Tab/Space=next · Enter=run · q/Esc=cancel · TTY-gated) driving `step_once`. Bead stays in_progress until the REPL Tab lands.

title: config: env-interp clobbers bare $vars in command: bash blocks
description:
Config env-interpolation (bd-b342fd) expands a BARE `$name` inside `command:` bash blocks, clobbering shell variables that collide with a SET env var. Concretely: aur-1's `@`/echo command uses bash `$out`; the nix dev-shell exports `out` (nix build output path), so config interp rewrites `$out` -> that path and the command breaks. Passes in clean CI where `out` is unset, so it is an environment-dependent flake (surfaced via eval::tests::command_realisation_runs_under_bash).

Options to consider (SPEC decision needed): (a) config interp only expands `${VAR}` braces, not bare `$name` (but SPEC line ~330 uses bare `$ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); (b) skip interpolation entirely for `command:` values and let bash do its own expansion; (c) only expand names that are explicitly config-referenced, leaving unknown `$name` literal; (d) require `$$` escaping in command blocks. Recommend (b): do not env-interpolate `command:` blocks. Owner: config lane (msm-0). Low urgency (env-specific) but a real footgun for anyone whose shell exports a colliding var.

title: [build] Linux x86 nix dev-shell coreutils segfaulting
description:
Reported by Harry (2026-07-03): on Linux x86, coreutils tools segfault inside the nlir `nix develop` dev shell, blocking `nix develop --command cargo build` for the Aurora agents.

Context / likely cause:
- nlir's flake pins nixpkgs to collective's exact rev (flakehub tarball rev 0590cd39) per operator request, to reuse the cached toolchain.
- The dev shell is only required on macOS to supply `libiconv` for the `-liconv` link (ureq/rustls TLS stack via feedback-cli/updatable-cli). On Linux, glibc already provides iconv.

WORKAROUND for Linux x86 (unblocks Aurora agents now):
- Build WITHOUT the dev shell: plain `cargo build` with a system Rust toolchain should link fine on Linux (no `-liconv` needed). Then `cargo fmt`/`clippy`/`test` directly.
- If a system Rust toolchain is unavailable, the nix rustc/cargo binaries themselves are fine; only coreutils in the interactive shell env is segfaulting — invoke tools by absolute path or use `nix build .#nlir` for a one-shot binary.

Diagnostics needed (from a Linux x86 node):
1. Is this nlir-specific or do OTHER cacophony project dev shells (omni-cli, tendril, collective) also segfault coreutils on the same node? If all projects, it is environmental (node/store), not the nlir flake.
2. Which exact coreutils tool + command segfaults (e.g. `cp`, `mkdir`, `cat`)? `dmesg`/exit signal?
3. Does `nix store verify --repair /nix/store/...coreutils...` or re-fetch fix it (partial/corrupt download)?
4. Does omni-cli (which pins github nixos-unstable, a NEWER nixpkgs) work on the same node? If yes, the pinned rev 0590cd39's coreutils is the culprit on that hardware and we may need a devShell nixpkgs bump for Linux.

Filed from the nlir config-vertical session (msm-0). Not claimed: needs a Linux x86 repro to fix.

MITIGATION LANDED (Harry, c08b7a9): removed `use flake` from `.envrc` so direnv no longer auto-loads the nix dev shell on `cd`. Practical build workflow now:
- Linux x86: build with the SYSTEM Rust toolchain — plain `cargo build` / `cargo test` / `cargo clippy` / `cargo fmt`. No dev shell needed (glibc provides iconv), which sidesteps the segfaulting dev-shell coreutils.
- macOS: still needs `libiconv`, so build via an explicit `nix develop --command cargo build` (the dev shell is fine on macOS; only Linux x86 coreutils segfaults).
Bead stays OPEN as a tracked known-issue: the underlying reason the pinned-rev dev-shell coreutils segfaults on Linux x86 is still undiagnosed and could resurface for `nix build`/CI. Diagnostics in the original description still wanted.

DIAGNOSTICS (aurora Linux x86_64, NixOS 6.18.32, nix 2.34.7; 2026-07-03, aur-2):

NOT REPRODUCIBLE on aurora now. Findings against the original questions:

1. nlir-specific vs environmental: NEITHER segfaults currently. nlir dev shell
   coreutils-9.8 (/nix/store/wkkwxc04gdw6b263l1h29pjarjnjdyb6-coreutils-9.8) AND
   omni-cli dev shell coreutils-9.11
   (/nix/store/9ypz3flqsrl5xl495mm8h645gadjsxi1-coreutils-9.11) both run
   cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm with exit=0.
2. Which tool segfaults: NONE currently -- cp/mkdir/cat/ls/mv/rm all exit=0 in
   the nlir dev shell.
3. store verify/repair: "nix store verify --no-trust <coreutils-9.8 path>"
   returns clean (exit 0) -- the path is intact, NOT corrupt now.
4. omni-cli (newer nixpkgs): works (coreutils-9.11). AND nlir's own pinned-rev
   coreutils-9.8 works too.

Also verified the original failing command path: "nix develop path:<nlir>
--command" runs cargo 1.91.0 + rustc 1.91.1 fine (TOOLCHAIN-OK).

CONCLUSION: The original segfault was almost certainly TRANSIENT store corruption
(a partial/corrupt download of the coreutils-9.8 store path) at report time,
since re-fetched/repaired -- the coreutils-9.8 path now verifies clean and works.
The pinned rev 0590cd39 is EXONERATED (a clean coreutils-9.8 works; no devShell
nixpkgs bump needed). The system-toolchain mitigation (c08b7a9) is the durable
Linux workflow and CI is green on it. Recommend resolving; reopen if the segfault
recurs (transient corruption can recur on a fresh partial download on any node).

title: Nested LLM operand in a & join leaks <text> wrapper tags (&['love',!'love'] => "love and <text>hate</text>")
description:
Dogfood bug (aur-2, found during golf): a nested LLM operator inside a `&`/list operand leaks `<text>...</text>` wrapper tags into the joined output.

Repro (--mode llm):
  &['love',!'love']  => "love and <text>hate</text>"   (expected: "love and hate")
  &['war',!'war']    => "war and <text>peace</text>"    (expected: "war and peace")

The inner !'love' correctly negates to the antonym "hate", but its LLM result comes back wrapped in <text>...</text> markup, and the & join splices that wrapper verbatim into the output instead of stripping it to the bare text. So any expression that JOINS an LLM-produced operand (e.g. &[literal, !x] or &[x, ~y]) can leak <text> tags.

Likely eval/realise lane (the realised operand isn't unwrapped before it feeds the next operator's prompt/join) — NOT config/types/llm (aur-2) or ranges (msm-0). Flagging for whoever owns the realise seam (adjacent to aur-1's <group> paren-echo work + msm-0's operand-realisation). Not filing into a specific lane; P3, cosmetic-but-real (blocks composing LLM ops through &/lists cleanly). Repro'd on origin/main @7b77a0d.

title: LLM-mode type coercion unimplemented: eval coerces operands deterministically only (types.model/prompt unused)
description:
Driving nlir in llm mode against a real Claude backend surfaced that LLM-mode type coercion is UNIMPLEMENTED. `nlir -e "'five' + 'three'"` (llm mode; `+` has operands: number) fails with `cannot coerce string \`five\` to number: no deterministic or model coercion produced a number`.

Root cause: `Evaluator::eval_apply` (src/eval.rs ~341-345 and the concurrent path ~612-614) coerces operands with `value.coerce(op_cfg.operands, &sep)` — DETERMINISTIC-only. The SPEC §Types llm fallback (config `types: number: { model, prompt, schema }`) is never invoked, so a non-numeric string can never be coerced via the model even in llm mode.

Fix: add an evaluator coercion that, when deterministic coercion fails AND mode==Llm AND config.types has the target type's model+prompt, calls the model (llm::realise_llm with the type's prompt over the rendered value) and parses the answer to the target type. String target still stringifies deterministically. Found by msm-0 while building the llm-mode working-cases library.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: det-mode echo of a multi-message range shows only the head element (0^*-1 -> first msg, not the span)
description:
DOGFOOD OBSERVATION (msm0), UX/display question — NOT a correctness bug, operators are unaffected.

FINDING: In det mode, echoing a multi-message range renders ONLY the head element, not the span.
  Context = 5 messages [picnic-plan, ..., db-incident-resolved].
  det:  0^*-1   => "Let's plan the company summer picnic."   (only msg 0)
        0^*2    => msg 0 only;  2^*4 => msg 2 only
  llm:  ~0^*-1  => "The team pivoted from planning a picnic to resolving a production DB emergency..."
        (correctly reflects ALL 5 messages)

So the RANGE itself is correct — LLM operators (~, #, etc.) receive the FULL span. This is confirmed:
#40 (selection), #50 (mission-control) are correct; they feed 0^*-1 to operators, which see everything.
It is ONLY the det-mode ECHO (the no-LLM passthrough render) that collapses a multi-message range to its
head element.

QUESTION for the realise/det-render lane (aur-1): is head-only the intended det echo of a multi-message
range, or should it join the messages (with a separator, like lists use _sep)? Currently `_sep=,;0^*2`
errors (parse), so there's no obvious way to see the whole range deterministically. A user doing `0^*-1`
in det mode to "see the whole conversation" gets only the first line silently, which is surprising.

OPTIONS: (a) intended — document that det echo of a range = its head (address resolves to start); (b) join
range messages in det echo using the list separator policy; (c) leave as-is but document. Low priority,
cosmetic — filed as a draft so the realise-lane owner can make the SPEC call, not rushed. Repro'd on
config.example.yaml @ current main.
creator: ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: M^N message-range is unreachable: range logic exists but no AST node / parser production / eval arm
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. The SPEC documents message ranges `M^N` (e.g. `(1+1)^(5+5)` = assistant messages 2..10, joined with _sep; worked-examples table row `#0^3;#^-1;&`). The RANGE LOGIC exists — messages.rs::MessageIndex::range(role,start,end,sep) landed in bd-43ac5e (closed) with tests — but it is DEAD CODE: nothing calls it.

Repro: `nlir -e '0^2' --context-file <ctx with assistant msgs> --mode det` → `parse error at token 1: unexpected token Message(Assistant) after statement`. The lexer emits `0` (Number) then `^2` (Message{Assistant,2}); the parser has no infix `^` handling so `M^N` never forms.

parser.rs:17-19 explicitly says the `M^N` message-range infix form is DEFERRED. So three pieces are missing:
1. AST: no `Expr::MessageRange { role, start, end }` node (only `Expr::Message{role,index}`).
2. Parser: no infix `^` production — must distinguish prefix `^N` (message index) from infix `M^N` (range) per the lexer.rs:801 note "parser distinguishes prefix vs infix `^`".
3. Eval: no arm to call MessageIndex::range (needs adding to both eval() and eval_parallel_safe(); it is read-only → parallel-safe).

Lanes: AST+parser = msm-0; eval+messages range logic = aur-1 (I wrote the range fn). Proposing aur-1 takes the whole vertical (small, and I own the range logic + eval) pending msm-0 OK on touching parser.rs, to avoid a multi-round handoff. Filed by aur-1.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: nlir power-move #25: the changelog :['ITEM',...] — terse notes to polished release notes (no-&=map, &=weave)
description:
nlir power-move #25 (aur-2) — "the changelog": turn terse dev notes into polished user-facing release notes — one clean line per item, in the register you choose. For changelogs, release notes, "what's new" emails, standup recaps. Uses a genuinely-distinct mechanism (per-item MAP, not a &-weave).

SHAPE: :['ITEM_1', 'ITEM_2', 'ITEM_3'] — a tone op on a list WITHOUT & MAPS over each item -> one rewritten line per item. : = friendly release notes, @ = formal changelog, ~ = terse.

KEY DISTINCTION verified (corrects a prior misread): a leading op on a list WITHOUT & MAPS (one line per item); WITH & it WEAVES into one sentence. The & is the weave.
  :['a','b','c'] -> three separate plain lines (changelog)
  :&['a','b','c'] -> one flowing sentence (summary)

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): :['fixed a crash on large uploads','sped up search 3x','added keyboard shortcuts'] ->
"Fixed a problem that made big file uploads stop working" / "Made searching much, much faster" / "Added shortcut keys you can press on the keyboard" (also verified @[...] gives a formal changelog, @&[...] weaves to one sentence)

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-changelog.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row (with the no-&=map / &=weave note), showcase card nlir-changelog.png (my 16th card; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row.

title: CLI: expression starting with '-' fails clap parsing (nlir -e '-1^*0') — needs allow_hyphen_values on --expr
description:
Dogfood finding (aur-0, while reconciling the range grammar with msm-0). An nlir expression passed to `-e` that STARTS with a `-` fails clap argument parsing instead of being treated as the expression:

  nlir -e '-1^*0'   => error: unexpected argument '-1' found
  nlir -e '-3^*-1'  => error: unexpected argument '-3' found
  nlir -e '-5'      => error: unexpected argument '-5' found

clap consumes the leading `-1`/`-3`/`-5` as an unknown flag before it reaches the expr value. This bites any expression that opens with a negative message index in a range form (e.g. `-1^*0` = a range from the last message), and any leading-negative token.

WORKAROUNDS (already functional): `nlir -e=-1^*0`, or `nlir -e -- -1^*0`, or `nlir -- -e '-1^*0'` depending on clap layout.

FIX: set `allow_hyphen_values(true)` (clap) on the `-e/--expr` arg (src/main.rs CLI definition) so a leading `-` in the expression value is not parsed as a flag. Verify it doesn't break real short flags. Consider the same for any other value-taking arg that legitimately accepts leading-`-` payloads.

Note: `-5` etc. are not valid nlir anyway (no unary-minus operator; negatives come from `0-5`), so the practical hit is mainly leading-negative-index range forms — a narrow but real CLI ergonomics paper-cut. No existing bead (searched hyphen / "unexpected argument" = 0). CLI lane = msm-0. Filed via reflect-session / dogfood from the range-grammar reconciliation.

title: Render nlir specification on GitHub Pages site
description:
Create and render the nlir (natural-language IR) specification documentation on the GitHub Pages site.

The specification should document:
- nlir as a natural-language IR system that transpiles terse, sigil-laden shorthand into fluent English
- The shorthand functioning as an intermediate representation that is tokenised and parsed into a DAG using a config-defined grammar
- Evaluation over a small stack machine with a tiny type system
- Two operator realisation modes: deterministic (mechanical string/number expansion) and LLM-based (structured text transformation via LLM call)

Acceptance Criteria:
- GitHub Pages site is configured to serve the specification
- nlir specification document is complete and accessible
- Documentation clearly explains the architecture: tokenisation → DAG parsing → stack machine evaluation → operator realisation

title: [bug] ** (pow) is left-associative — 2**3**2 = 64, should be 512 (right-assoc per SPEC "normal math")
description:
Found dogfooding in det mode (aur-0, no network). Exponentiation `**` parses as LEFT-associative, but mathematical convention (and Python) make it RIGHT-associative, and the SPEC says "binary follows normal math — ** > * / > + -".

Reproduction (all via `nlir --config config.example.yaml --mode det --quiet`):
- `2**3**2` => 64   (left: (2**3)**2 = 8**2 = 64; expected right: 2**(3**2) = 2**9 = 512)
- `2**1**3` => 8    (left: (2**1)**3 = 8; expected right: 2**(1**3) = 2)
- `4**3**0` => 1    (left: (4**3)**0 = 1; expected right: 4**(3**0) = 4)

Correct for comparison (left-assoc IS right for these): `10-4-3 => 3`, `16/4/2 => 2` — subtraction and division are correctly left-associative.

Root cause: src/parser.rs treats ALL binary/infix/mixfix operators as uniformly left-associative. See the module doc comment (lines ~10-12): "infix (`- /`) is binary, left-associative; mixfix (`& | + *`) is treated as left-associative binary here". `pow` (`op: **`, fixity: infix, arity: 2) inherits this uniform left-assoc, so chained `**` groups left instead of right. The binding-power helper already notes it doubles priority "so left-associativity has room" — i.e. right-assoc is a (right-binding-power - 1) adjustment away.

Proposed fix (config-idiomatic, keeps "the language is config"):
1. Add an optional `assoc: left|right` field to the operator config struct in src/config.rs (default `left`; validate the enum).
2. Set `assoc: right` on the `pow` operator in config.example.yaml (and the SPEC's example config).
3. In src/parser.rs Pratt loop: for a right-associative infix op at priority p, recurse the right operand with min binding power (2p - 1) instead of 2p, so equal-precedence continues rightward.
4. SPEC.md: add an explicit associativity note (binary ops left-associative by default; `**` right-associative), since the Grammar/precedence section currently omits associativity.
5. Tests: parser unit test for `2**3**2` right-grouping; a config `tests:` case `pow-right-assoc: { mode: det, expr: "2**3**2", expected: "512" }`.

Cross-lane note: parser = msm-0 lane, config schema = aur-2 lane. Filed by aur-0; will coordinate before landing. Low frequency in practice (chained ** is rare) but a clear spec-conformance gap. No existing bead (searched associativ/exponent = 0).

title: Leading-dot float literals (.5) do not lex — decide if .5 should parse as 0.5
description:
Reflect-session dogfood observation (aur-0, from the bd-df62f1 session). After bd-f551f9 (float literals) was fixed, bare floats with an integer part lex fine (0.5, 3.14, 2**0.5). Two remaining decimal-point cases still error:
- `.5` (leading dot, no integer part) => lex error at position 0: unexpected character '.'
- `3.` (trailing dot) => lex error at position 1

`3.` and `1.2.3` (double dot) rejections are INTENTIONAL per the bd-f551f9 close note ("reject a bare trailing/double dot"). The only arguable gap is leading-dot `.5`, which many languages accept as 0.5. This is a minor convenience, and may well be intentionally excluded too — filing as a low-pri draft so lexer-lane triage (msm-0) can decide whether `.5` should lex as 0.5 or stay rejected. Not blocking anything; `0.5` already works.

Lexer lane = msm-0. Filed via reflect-session from bd-df62f1 session.

title: parallelism CLI flag resolved then dropped: override never reaches the scheduler
description:
Follow-up to bd-149949 (msm-0's note: "--parallelism 1 did not force sequential in the sleep test — flag->scheduler wiring gap"). Confirmed + fixed.

Root cause: run_eval / run_repl call resolve_defaults(&cfg, &cli_overrides(cli)) which DOES resolve the CLI parallelism override into settings.parallelism, but only settings.mode is passed to evaluate(). The Evaluator reads its parallelism from config.defaults.parallelism (the frozen evaluate(expr,&Config,&mut Context,Mode) signature has no parallelism param), so settings.parallelism is computed then silently dropped -- the CLI flag had no effect.

Fix (main.rs resolve_config, the single choke point all eval paths share): after loading, apply cfg.defaults.parallelism = cli.parallelism.max(1) when set, so the override reaches the scheduler via the config the Evaluator already reads. Respects the frozen evaluate() signature (no API change).

Verified with a sleep-2s command operator, ~a&~b&~c: default(8)=2s (concurrent), parallelism 1=6s (sequential), parallelism 2=4s (chunked 2+1). The parallelism=1->sequential eval behaviour is already locked by the det_seq cache test. Found+fixed by aur-1 while driving nlir in llm mode.

title: Lexer: `_` echo operator can't tokenise (lex_bare consumes `_` mid-token)
description:
The SPEC `_` echo operator (op: "_", the det-echo test `xxx_2` → "xxx xxx") cannot be tokenised. lex_bare (src/lexer.rs ~line 369) treats `_` as a bare-token continuation char (`c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_'`) — a consequence of allowing `_`-prefixed system keys like `_sep`. So `xxx_2` lexes as a single Bare("xxx_2") and the configured `_` operator never splits it; the evaluator returns the input unchanged.

Repro: with a config operator `echo: { op: "_", arity: 2, fixity: infix, command: ... }`, `nlir -e 'xxx_2'` (det) yields "xxx_2" instead of "xxx xxx".

This blocks SPEC det-echo. The fix is in the lexer lane (msm-0): disambiguate the `_` echo operator from `_`-in-bare-token / `_`-prefixed keys — e.g. only treat leading `_` as a bare/key start (not a mid-token continuation), or let a configured operator sigil `_` win when it appears between operands, while `_name` at a key position still lexes as a key. Command realisation itself (bd-3c1e6d) is verified working via a non-`_` sigil in src/eval.rs tests; only the `_`-sigil lexing is blocked. Filed by aur-1 from the eval SPEC-completion batch (bd-c85dee/bd-02a795/bd-3c1e6d).
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1
spoken_name: lexer underscore echo operator tokenization bug

title: Parser: stack overflow on deeply-nested input (fuzz-found) — add recursion depth guard
description:
A fuzz test (no-panic corpus over tokenize+parse) found that the recursive-descent Pratt parser has NO recursion-depth bound, so deeply-nested input (e.g. `nlir -e '(((((…a…)))))'` ~256+ deep, or nested `[a,[a,…`) recurses until the stack overflows → SIGABRT (exit 134), crashing the CLI instead of returning a clean error. DoS-class robustness bug.

Fix: added `MAX_PARSE_DEPTH = 256` and a depth guard on the `expr` recursion chokepoint (Parser gains a `depth` field; `expr` increments/checks/decrements around the renamed `expr_inner`). All recursion (groups `(`, lists `[`, prefix ops, backtick, message index) routes through the guarded `expr`, so deeper nesting now returns `parse error: expression nesting too deep` instead of overflowing. Verified: depth 100 → ok, depth 256/300/2000 → clean error (exit 1). 256 is safely below both the ~2MB test-thread and 8MB main-thread overflow points. Added `fuzz_tokenize_and_parse_never_panic` (deterministic xorshift corpus, no proptest dep) to lock the no-panic guarantee. Filed + fixed by msm-0.
creator: ms-mac:nlir:ms-mac-nlir-nlir-dev-msm-0

title: Bare float literals do not lex: decimal point rejected (3.14, 0.5, 2**0.5)
description:
Found while driving nlir in LLM mode. Bare decimal/float number literals fail to lex:
- `nlir -e '3.14'` => lex error at position 1: unexpected character '.'
- `nlir -e '0.5'` => same
- `nlir -e '2**0.5'` => lex error on the '.'
- `nlir -e '1.5+2.5'` => same

But float RESULTS work fine (`7/2` => 3.5) and QUOTED decimals coerce (`'3.14'+'2.86'` => 6). So you can COMPUTE floats but cannot WRITE them as literals — an asymmetry confirming this is a gap, not intended integer-only.

Root: lexer.rs emits numbers as `Token::Bare(digit-run)` (as_number accepts `s.bytes().all(is_ascii_digit)`), and the standalone number scanner does not consume a `.`+fractional part, so `.` hits the "unexpected character / not a configured sigil" path. Fix: extend the bare-number lexing to accept an optional single `.` + fractional digits (and reject a bare trailing/double dot), producing a Token::Number(f64) or a float-parseable Bare. Watch the interaction with `^N.M`? (no — ranges are M^N, not dotted) and with `_sep`/list commas. Lexer lane = msm-0. Filed by aur-1.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-1

title: CLI: context-source precedence
description:
Parent epic bd-bc848a (label `cli`). `--context-file` > `--session-file` > env > default file.

[note from aur-1, bd-0909ab context store landing] The precedence LOGIC now lives in `nlir::context::Context::load(LoadSources, &ContextConfig)` (src/context.rs). This CLI bead is the wiring layer: resolve the ambient env into a `LoadSources` and call `Context::load`. Semantics the store already implements (strict first-present-wins): `--context-file` > session import > `NLIR_CONTEXT` env > default file. Write-through targets: `--context-file` and the default file are file-backed; `NLIR_CONTEXT` env and session import are TRANSIENT (in-memory only, no write-through). Session-file *format parsing* is NOT this bead nor the store — it belongs to the sessions epic (bd-720cdb parse → Map, bd-000666 merge); that Map is handed to `LoadSources.session`. `default_context_path(&cfg, HOME)` + `expand_tilde` resolve `context.file_default` (tilde-expanded). SPEC clarity gap surfaced while implementing: the SPEC §CLI list writes "--session-file merge" which could read as merge-on-top-of-lower-precedence rather than strict-precedence; the store chose strict first-present-wins — confirm that's the intended reading when wiring this.

title: nlir power-move #19: the question set ['ASSUMPTION'?,...] — flip assumptions into the questions to ask (fresh ? operator)
description:
nlir power-move #19 (aur-2) — "the question set": turn your nagging assumptions into the pointed questions to ask BEFORE committing. A genuinely-fresh move on a previously-UNTOUCHED operator: ? (postfix) flips a statement into a question. Nobody in the swarm had built a ?-based move.

SHAPE: ['ASSUMPTION_1'?, 'ASSUMPTION_2'?, ...] — a list; ? is POSTFIX (goes AFTER each string; [?'x'] is a parse error "? not valid in prefix position"). Each ? rewrites the statement as the well-formed question that tests it, one per line. Lead with @ to formalize each question, : to keep them plain.

Real intent: you think in assumptions, the people you need answers from think in questions — ? does the flip. Due-diligence / kickoff / code-review / risk-assessment checklist.

Filled + REAL (claude-sonnet-5): ['the timeline is fixed'?,'the budget covers a full rewrite'?,'the team has the bandwidth'?] ->
"Is the timeline fixed?" / "Does the budget cover a full rewrite?" / "Does the team have the bandwidth?" (and @[...] formalizes: "Does the team have sufficient bandwidth to undertake this effort?")

Deliverables: examples/move-aur2-questions.sh (runs nlir live, --examples gate exit 0), POWERMOVES.md quick-ref row, showcase card nlir-question-set.png (my 9th card; single --only regen, honest llm pill) + scripts/build-showcase.py row. Fresh operator (?), distinct from all swarm moves.
creator: aurora:nlir:aurora-nlir-nlir-dev-aur-2
